serving.natural_language_portion: One medium glazed donut weighing approximately 60 g, as commonly sold at bakeries and donut chains.

nutrient_source.title: Doughnuts, yeast-leavened, glazed, enriched (includes honey buns) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 18249
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for yeast-leavened glazed doughnut is an exact match for this food. Singapore HPB values (energy 432 kcal, protein 6.73 g, fat 25.59 g, carbohydrate 43.56 g, sugar 17.65 g, sodium 361 mg, dietary fibre 1.33 g) are preserved from the input as the primary HPB canonical record. USDA SR Legacy is used to supplement saturated fat, cholesterol, and micronutrient data not reported by HPB. USDA SR Legacy reports: energy 391 kcal, protein 6.26 g, fat 21.0 g, carbohydrate 44.9 g, sugar 17.1 g, sodium 326 mg, dietary fibre 1.5 g per 100 g — values are consistent in profile with HPB figures, confirming plausibility. HPB values are used for all primary macros; USDA SR Legacy used for saturated fat and micronutrients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 76 (±4) is reported for a cake-type doughnut in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Yeast-leavened glazed doughnuts have a similar refined-flour, high-sugar composition and are expected to have a comparable or slightly higher GI. GI 76 is used as a defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (76 × 43.56 × 0.60) / 100 = (76 × 26.14) / 100 ≈ 19.9, rounded to 20.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (17.65 g/100 g) with low fibre (1.33 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 361 mg/100 g is elevated; saturated and trans fat content raises cardiovascular risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid glazed donuts; the combination of high sugar, elevated sodium (361 mg/100 g), saturated fat (~5.8 g/100 g), and trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g) independently and synergistically elevates blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~76), high sugar (17.65 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g), and refined carbohydrate base (43.56 g/100 g) produce rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; trans fat worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Glazed donuts are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high GI (~76), high sugar load (17.65 g/100 g), minimal fibre (1.33 g/100 g), and trans fat content cause rapid blood glucose spikes and impair insulin sensitivity — avoid entirely or limit to rare, small portions with careful glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.8 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g), high sugar (17.65 g/100 g), and elevated sodium (361 mg/100 g) collectively raise LDL cholesterol, promote endothelial inflammation, and increase atherosclerotic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Glazed donuts are high-concern for cardiovascular disease; the combination of saturated fat, trans fat, high sugar, and elevated sodium raises LDL cholesterol and promotes vascular inflammation — regular consumption should be avoided by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A glazed donut (also spelled glazed doughnut) is a deep-fried, yeast-leavened ring of enriched wheat dough coated with a thin, sweet sugar glaze that sets to a shiny, slightly crisp finish. The interior is soft, airy, and mildly sweet, while the glaze adds a concentrated sweetness and a delicate crunch. Originating in the United States and now widely consumed globally — including in Singapore as a popular bakery and café snack — glazed donuts are typically eaten as a breakfast item or snack. Nutritionally, a glazed donut is energy-dense at 432 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (25.59 g/100 g, including ~5.8 g saturated fat and ~0.3 g trans fat from shortening), high sugar (17.65 g/100 g), moderate protein (6.73 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g). Sodium is elevated at 361 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is approximately 76 (high), making this food a rapid driver of postprandial blood glucose elevation. Clinically, glazed donuts are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to the combination of high GI, high sugar, trans fat, saturated fat, and elevated sodium. The deep-frying process also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory. This food is not recommended for regular consumption in health-conscious or clinical dietary contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a glazed donut?
Can a diabetic eat a glazed donut?
Is glazed donut high in sugar?
Is glazed doughnut suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in a glazed donut?
Can I eat a glazed donut if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Primary macros sourced from Singapore HPB canonical database (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, micronutrients, and trans fat supplemented from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 18249), which is an exact match for yeast-leavened glazed doughnut. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008 (peer-reviewed). All values are internally consistent and cross-validated between HPB and USDA SR Legacy.
notes: 1. Primary macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced from Singapore HPB canonical database as provided in the input record (confidence 0.95). 2. Saturated fat (5.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (11.4 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (6.5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.3 g/100 g), and all micronutrients are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 18249 (yeast-leavened glazed doughnut), which is an exact match. 3. Trans fat value of 0.3 g/100 g is an estimate from USDA SR Legacy for this food category; actual trans fat content varies by manufacturer and shortening type. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1: detailed vitamin D data for glazed donut is not consistently reported in USDA SR Legacy or HPB database as of 2026. 5. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cake-type doughnut, Atkinson et al. 2008); yeast-leavened glazed doughnuts are expected to have a comparable GI. 6. Input dietary_tags included 'plant_based' and 'wholegrain' — both are incorrect for a glazed donut (contains eggs and milk; made from refined flour) and have been removed. 7. Halal status is not confirmed as shortening source and gelatin use vary by manufacturer; is_halal set to false pending label verification. 8. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
